supplementary.title: [For Student] Biographies of people known for reliability and trustworthiness

competence_markers.description: They keep commitments reliably — stated promises are fulfilled on time and to standard in the clear majority of cases
They speak up in groups when they see problems — they do not wait silently for failure and then disclaim responsibility
They own their contribution to group outcomes, including negative ones, without deflection or blame-shifting
They maintain a commitment log and review it honestly, identifying gaps between what they promised and what they delivered
They demonstrate awareness of their reputation — they can articulate what their pattern of behavior communicates to others
They respond to failures with repair plans rather than excuses — the default response is "here is what I am doing to fix it," not "here is why it was not my fault"
They understand the distinction between intention and impact and do not use good intentions as a shield against accountability for bad outcomes
They participate honestly in after-action reviews, identifying their own failures without prompting

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it mean to keep your word?
narration_prompts.detail: Why does it matter? Can you think of a time you kept a commitment even when it was inconvenient? What about a time you did not — and what happened?
How is it built? How is it damaged? What is yours, honestly? What would the people around you say about your reliability?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a time a group you were part of failed at something.
narration_prompts.detail: What was your specific contribution to the result — both what went well and what went wrong? What did you learn?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between an excuse and a response?
narration_prompts.detail: When you fail at something, which do you tend to give? Can you give an example?
How is it damaged? Can you think of someone you trust completely — and explain why? Can you think of someone who lost your trust — and explain what happened?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time someone was counting on you and you let them down.
narration_prompts.detail: Not a story about someone else. A story about you. What happened? What did you do about it? What would you do differently now?

cross_connections.description: You cannot keep commitments you cannot manage. The planning skills of IC1.1-B are the operational infrastructure that makes IC1.3-B's accountability possible. A student who is accountable in principle but disorganized in practice will still break commitments — the cause is different, but the impact is the same.

Accountability is the first and non-negotiable requirement of leadership. Everything in IC1.3-B — keeping your word, owning group outcomes, putting consequences over excuses — directly prepares for the leadership accountability of Domain 4.3.

In business, your reputation is your most valuable intangible asset. The trust mechanics of IC1.3-B — how trust compounds, how it is destroyed, how it is rebuilt — are directly applicable to every business relationship.

Group accountability at IC1.3-B scales to civic accountability at 4.5. The citizen who understands that their contribution to community outcomes is real — not symbolic — is practicing IC1.3 at a larger scale.

Accountability (IC1.3) and failure recovery (IC1.4) are deeply linked at Weathering Storms. The student who can own a failure without collapsing into shame is practicing both sub-domains. IC1.4 provides the emotional resilience; IC1.3 provides the behavioral framework.

In the trades, your work is your word made visible. The joint that is tight, the weld that is clean, the measurement that is precise — these are integrity in physical form. The craftsperson whose work cannot be trusted has no career. This is IC1.3 expressed through the hands.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned to tell the truth, own your mistakes, and make things right. Those habits were built inside your family — the smallest circle of people who know you. The consequences were personal and immediate: you broke something, you confessed, you fixed it.

Weathering Storms expands the circle. You are now old enough to be part of groups — teams, classes, churches, clubs, neighborhoods, projects. In groups, your actions do not only affect you. They affect people who are counting on you. When you say you will do something and you do not do it, someone else either covers for you or the thing does not get done. When you lie to a group, the group makes decisions based on your lie. When you are unreliable, people stop relying on you — and that is not a punishment. It is a rational response to the evidence you have given them about who you are.

This packet is about understanding that your word is not just a sound you make. It is a debt. When you say "I will be there," you owe being there. When you say "I will finish it," you owe finishing it. When you say "I did my part," the truth of that statement is about to be tested by the people who depended on it.

You are also going to learn about something you have been building since Stretching Branches without fully realizing it: your reputation. Your reputation is what people believe about you based on your pattern of behavior — not what you say about yourself, not what you intend, but what you actually do, consistently, over time. It is the most valuable thing you own that you cannot buy, cannot fake, and cannot borrow. You can only build it, slowly, or destroy it, quickly. Most people your age are not thinking about reputation. That is why most people your age are building theirs carelessly. You will not make that mistake.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Accountability for group outcomes** — understanding that when you are part of a group, the group's result is partly your responsibility, including when things go wrong
2. **Keeping your word** — treating a stated commitment as a real obligation, not a casual intention subject to revision when it becomes inconvenient
3. **Reputation awareness** — understanding that trust is built slowly and lost quickly, and that your pattern of behavior creates a picture of you in other people's minds that you cannot directly control
4. **Trust mechanics** — how trust is built, what damages it, how long it takes to rebuild after real damage, and why trustworthiness is one of the most strategically valuable traits a person can have
5. **Consequences over excuses** — learning that the quality of your explanation for a failure matters far less than the quality of your response to it
6. **After-action accountability** — the habit of reviewing what happened, identifying your role honestly, and determining what you owe to those affected
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Your Word Is a Debt

When you tell someone you will do something, you have created an obligation. Not a hope. Not an intention. An obligation. The other person is now operating on the assumption that the thing will be done. They may have made their own plans based on your commitment. They may have assigned other work to other people because they believed your part was covered. They may have told a third person that it was handled — on your word.

If you do not follow through, the cost is not just the undone task. It is the cascade of everything that was built on the assumption that you would do what you said. This is why unreliability is so expensive — it does not just create one problem. It creates problems in every direction the broken commitment touched.

**Examples you can feel:**

- You tell your team you will bring the supplies for Saturday's project. You forget. Saturday morning, the whole team is standing there with no supplies. The project cannot happen. Every person's time is wasted — not because of a difficult obstacle, but because you said you would do something and did not do it.
- You commit to finishing your part of a group presentation by Thursday. Thursday comes and your part is not done. The person presenting after you cannot make transitions to material that does not exist. The group looks unprepared. Everyone carries the consequence. You carry the cause.
- You promise your younger sibling you will play with them after lunch. After lunch, a friend calls and you leave. The sibling waited — they turned down other things because they were counting on your promise. You taught them that your word is not reliable.

None of these examples involve malice. You did not intend harm. But **intent is not the measure of accountability. Impact is.** The people affected by your broken commitment do not experience your good intentions. They experience the absence of what you promised.

**The practice:** Before you commit to anything, pause. Ask yourself: "Am I actually going to do this? Do I have the time, the resources, and the will to follow through?" If the answer is no, say no. A clear "no" is infinitely more valuable than a "yes" that evaporates. People can plan around your "no." They cannot plan around your unreliable "yes."

And when you commit, record it. Write it down. Track it. At the end of each week, review: what did I say I would do? What did I actually do? Where is the gap? Why?

### Part 2: Accountability in Groups

Until now, most of your accountability has been personal — between you and your parent, you and your mentor. Weathering Storms introduces group accountability, which operates differently and at higher stakes.

In a group, you are not just responsible for your own behavior. You are responsible for your contribution to the group's outcome. This is an uncomfortable idea for people who prefer to think of responsibility as purely individual: "I did my part. It is not my fault the result was bad." But in a group, your part does not exist in isolation. It connects to every other part. If your part fails, other parts may fail because of it. If your part succeeds but you saw another part failing and said nothing, you share in the failure.

**Group accountability means:**

- **You do your part, fully and on time.** Not mostly. Not late. Not "good enough." To the standard the group needs, when the group needs it.
- **You speak up when you see a problem.** If you notice that the plan is flawed, that someone's part is incomplete, or that the group is heading for failure, you say so — even if it is uncomfortable, even if it makes you unpopular. Silence in the face of a visible problem is a choice, and it is a choice that makes you complicit in the outcome.
- **You own your role in failures.** After something goes wrong, you do not point fingers. You start with yourself: "What was my contribution to this result?" Even if your contribution was small — even if the primary failure was someone else's — you identify and own your part.
- **You do not take credit you did not earn.** When the group succeeds, the credit belongs to the group. If your contribution was smaller than others', you do not pretend otherwise. If someone else carried the weight, you acknowledge it.

**The after-action review** is the formal practice of group accountability. After any significant group effort — a project, an event, a competition, a task — the group sits down and answers three questions honestly:
1. What happened? (Factual. What was the plan? What actually occurred?)
2. What was each person's contribution — including where they fell short?
3. What do we owe to anyone who was affected by our result, and what will we do differently next time?

This is not a blame session. It is an honesty session. Its purpose is learning and repair, not punishment. But it requires a kind of honesty that many people — including many adults — find extremely difficult: the willingness to say, in front of others, "I did not do what I said I would do, and here is the impact."

### Part 3: Reputation — The Compound Interest of Character

You have a reputation. You may not have thought about it in those terms, but everyone who knows you has an idea of who you are — and that idea was not formed by what you say about yourself. It was formed by what you do.

Your reputation is the sum of your observed behavior over time. It answers questions that people do not ask aloud but always ask internally:
- Can I count on this person?
- Do they do what they say they will do?
- Do they tell the truth?
- When things go wrong, do they own it or hide it?
- Are they the same person when no one is watching?

You do not get to decide your reputation directly. You can only influence it through your actions. And the math is not equal: **trust is built in drops and lost in gallons.** A hundred kept promises build a reputation for reliability. One broken promise at a critical moment can damage it severely. This is not fair. But it is real, and understanding it gives you an enormous advantage over people who do not.

**Reputation compounds.** The person who is consistently reliable gets given more responsibility — because they can be trusted with it. More responsibility means more opportunity. More opportunity means more growth. The person who is unreliable gets given less — not as punishment, but as rational allocation. Why would anyone assign a critical task to someone who has shown they will not complete it?

Over years, this compounding effect produces dramatically different life trajectories for people of similar talent. The talented but unreliable person is consistently passed over. The less talented but reliable person is consistently trusted with more. Talent matters. Reliability matters more, because talent without reliability is a liability — it produces occasional brilliance surrounded by consistent disappointment.

**What this means for you now:** Every commitment you keep, every truth you tell, every mistake you own is an investment in your reputation. You may not see the return immediately. That is how compounding works — it is invisible in the short term and overwhelming in the long term. The twelve-year-old who keeps their word builds a reputation that the sixteen-year-old benefits from. The sixteen-year-old's reputation determines what the twenty-year-old is trusted with. Start now.

### Part 4: Trust Mechanics — How Trust Works

Trust is not a feeling. It is a prediction. When someone trusts you, they are predicting — based on your history of behavior — that you will do what you say, tell the truth, and behave consistently whether or not you are observed. The prediction is based on evidence, not on hope.

**How trust is built:**
- **Consistency.** Doing what you say you will do, repeatedly, over time. Not once — not twice — but as a pattern. Each instance of follow-through adds evidence to the prediction.
- **Truth-telling under cost.** Anyone can tell the truth when it is easy. Trust is built by telling the truth when it costs something — when the truth makes you look bad, when the truth creates a consequence you would rather avoid.
- **Transparency.** Letting people see your process, not just your results. When you struggle, saying so. When you are behind, saying so before the deadline, not after. When you do not know, admitting it rather than faking it.
- **Restitution after failure.** When you fail (and you will), the speed and quality of your repair matters more than the failure itself. The person who fails, owns it immediately, and makes repair builds more trust through the failure than the person who succeeds quietly without being tested.

**How trust is damaged:**
- **Broken commitments.** Saying you will do something and not doing it. The severity scales with the importance of the commitment and the dependency others placed on it.
- **Lies.** Any lie, of any size, damages trust — because the discovery of one lie makes every prior statement uncertain. "If they lied about this, what else have they lied about?" This is why small lies are not small. They create a shadow over everything.
- **Inconsistency.** Being reliable when observed and unreliable when alone. Being honest with some people and dishonest with others. Being accountable when the stakes are high and careless when they seem low. Inconsistency is its own form of dishonesty.
- **Excuse-making.** Habitual excuses — "It was not my fault," "I could not help it," "You do not understand the situation" — erode trust because they signal that the person will not own their behavior. The excuse may be true. But a pattern of excuses, even true ones, builds the reputation of someone who deflects rather than owns.

**How trust is rebuilt after damage:**
Slowly. With consistent changed behavior over time. Not with a single dramatic apology. Not with promises. With sustained, observable, verified action that demonstrates the old pattern has been replaced by a new one.

Rebuilding trust takes significantly longer than building it in the first place — because the person you damaged now has evidence that you are capable of the damaging behavior. They are not just building a new prediction. They are overwriting an old one. This takes time, patience, and sustained reliability with no guarantee that the other person will ever fully trust you again. That is the real cost of broken trust, and it is a cost worth understanding before you incur it.

### Part 5: Consequences Over Excuses

When something goes wrong — when you fail to deliver, when you miss a commitment, when your part of the group effort is not done — there is a moment where you choose between two responses:

**Response one: The excuse.** "I could not get to it because..." "It was not entirely my fault because..." "You have to understand that..." The excuse explains why the failure happened. It distributes cause. It provides context. And it does absolutely nothing to fix the problem.

**Response two: The response.** "It is not done. Here is what I am doing to fix it. Here is when it will be done. Here is what I am doing to make sure it does not happen again." The response acknowledges the failure, takes ownership, and moves immediately to repair.

**Consequences over excuses** means that the quality of your response to a failure matters infinitely more than the quality of your explanation for it. The person who delivers a brilliant explanation and no repair is worse than the person who delivers no explanation and immediate repair. The world does not grade you on the elegance of your reasons. It grades you on results and on what you do when results fall short.

This does not mean explanations are never appropriate. If someone asks what happened, tell them — honestly, briefly, without self-pity. But the explanation comes after the repair plan, not instead of it. And if you find that your explanations are consistently longer than your repair actions, that itself is diagnostic: you are spending more energy justifying the failure than fixing it.

**The practice:** When you fall short of a commitment, lead with three things:
1. "It is not done." (Acknowledgment. No softening.)
2. "Here is what I am doing about it." (Repair plan. Specific.)
3. "Here is what changes going forward." (Prevention. Concrete.)

That is it. No extended backstory. No emotional processing aloud. No fishing for sympathy. Own it, fix it, change it. If someone wants the explanation, they will ask. Give it briefly and honestly. Then return to the repair.

### Part 6: The Gap Between Intention and Impact

One of the most important things you will learn in this packet — and one of the hardest — is that your intentions do not matter as much as you think they do.

You did not mean to let the team down. You did not intend to break your promise. You were not trying to hurt anyone. All of that may be completely true. And none of it changes the impact of what actually happened. The team was let down. The promise was broken. Someone was hurt.

**Intentions are private. Impact is public.** The people affected by your actions experience the impact, not the intention. They do not feel your good heart. They feel the absence of what you promised. They do not hear your internal narrative about how busy you were. They hear the silence where your completed work was supposed to be.

This is not a call to disregard intention entirely. Intentions matter for your own moral accounting — the person who fails despite genuine effort is in a very different moral position than the person who fails from indifference. But to the people you affect, the distinction is less meaningful than you want it to be. They needed the thing done. It was not done. The reason it was not done is secondary to the fact of it.

Understanding this gap between intention and impact is one of the marks of moral maturity. The immature person says: "But I did not mean to!" and expects that to resolve the situation. The mature person says: "I did not mean to, and the impact happened anyway, and I own the impact regardless of my intention."
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Commitment Tracking [Daily/Weekly]
Maintain your commitment log. Every time you make a promise — to family, friends, mentors, or groups — write it down. At the end of each week, review: what did I commit to? What did I deliver? Where is the gap? Why? This is not a guilt exercise. It is a calibration exercise. The goal is to make your commitments match your behavior — either by improving your follow-through or by committing more carefully.

### Practice 2: After-Action Reviews [After Group Efforts]
After any significant group endeavor — a project, an event, a team task — conduct an after-action review. What happened? What was my specific contribution? Where did I fall short? What do I owe to those affected? What will I do differently? These can be conducted formally with the group or privately in your journal. Both have value.

### Practice 3: The Commitment Pause [Ongoing]
Before committing to anything, pause. Ask: "Am I actually going to do this? Do I have the time and resources? Am I saying yes because I want to, or because I feel pressured to?" If you cannot commit honestly, say no. Practice the clear "no" — it is a skill that protects your reputation far more effectively than the unfulfilled "yes."

### Practice 4: Public Commitments With Follow-Up [Monthly]
Once a month, make a commitment publicly — to your family, your mentor, your team. State what you will do and by when. Then, at the stated time, report back: did you do it? If not, what happened, and what is the repair plan? This practice builds the habit of public accountability and the discipline of follow-through under observation.

### Practice 5: Reputation Reflection [Quarterly, With Mentor]
Every three months, sit with your mentor and discuss: "What do people think of me? What would they say about my reliability, my honesty, my follow-through? Is that picture accurate? Is it the picture I want?" This is not about image management. It is about honest self-assessment of the reputation you are building through your actual behavior.

### Practice 6: Repair Practice [As Needed]
When you damage trust — and you will — practice the repair sequence: acknowledge the specific damage, take ownership without deflection, make restitution where possible, and commit to changed behavior. Then: actually change the behavior. Track this in your commitment log. The repair is not complete when the words are spoken. It is complete when the changed behavior is sustained.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Creating Opportunities for Group Accountability

IC1.3-B cannot be formed in isolation. The student needs repeated experience being part of a group where their contribution matters — where their absence is felt, where their failure to deliver creates a real cost for others, and where their reliability (or lack of it) is observed.

This means you must ensure the student is in contexts that require group accountability:
- Team projects where tasks are divided and each person's output is visible
- Collaborative activities where failure to prepare means the group suffers
- Service commitments where other people are depending on them to show up
- Any context where "I did not feel like it" is not an acceptable reason for non-performance, because others were counting on them

These contexts should be real, not simulated. A contrived group project designed to teach accountability teaches nothing — the student can smell the artificial stakes. A real commitment to a real group with real consequences is the formation environment. If the student is part of a sports team, a theater production, a volunteer crew, a building project, a homeschool co-op, or any group with shared work, that is the formation context. If they are not, create one — but make it real.

### Handling Excuses vs. Explanations

At Weathering Storms, the student will begin producing sophisticated excuses. Unlike the Stretching Branches child who denies or hides, the Weathering Storms student explains. At length. With plausibility. The distinction between an excuse and a legitimate explanation is not in the content — it is in the pattern and the response.

**Legitimate explanation:** "I did not finish the project because I got sick on Wednesday and Thursday. Here is what I have done so far, and here is my plan to complete it by Friday." (Specific cause. Already in motion on the repair. Takes ownership of the remaining obligation.)

**Excuse:** "I did not finish the project because I had so much going on this week and it was really hard and I did not have enough time and also I was not feeling great." (Vague causes. No repair plan. Seeking sympathy rather than offering a solution.)

The tell is what comes after the explanation. If the student moves immediately to repair — "here is what I am doing about it" — the explanation was informational. If the student stops after the explanation and waits for the consequence to be removed — they were making an excuse.

Your response: "I hear the explanation. What is the plan?" Every time. Redirect from cause to response. Over time, the student learns that the explanation is noted but the repair is what matters.

### The Commitment Log as Formation Tool

The commitment log is a simple practice with enormous diagnostic value. The student records:
1. What they committed to
2. To whom
3. By when
4. Whether they delivered
5. If not, what happened and what they did about it

Review this weekly. Not as an inquisition — as a conversation. "Let us look at your commitments this week. Where did you deliver? Where did you fall short? What patterns do you notice?"

The patterns are where the formation happens. A student who consistently over-commits learns to say no more carefully. A student who consistently delivers late learns to build in margin. A student who delivers to authority figures but not to peers learns something about whose expectations they take seriously — and that is worth discussing. A student who commits and delivers reliably is building something they can see accumulating in the log: evidence of their own trustworthiness.

Do not use the log punitively. If it becomes a weapon — "Look at all the things you did not do!" — the student will stop recording honestly, and the tool dies. Use it diagnostically, with genuine curiosity about the student's patterns, and celebrate the trajectory even when the numbers are not perfect.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Keeping Commitments

**Developing well:** The student fulfills stated commitments in the clear majority of cases. When they cannot, they notify early — before the deadline, not after — and present a repair plan. Their commitment log shows a trend of increasing reliability.

**Developing slowly:** Commitments are frequently broken. The student commits easily but does not follow through. There is no pattern of pre-deadline notification — failures are discovered after the fact. The commitment log (if maintained at all) shows a persistent gap between stated and delivered.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student over-committing? Some students say yes to everything because they cannot tolerate the discomfort of saying no. If so, the intervention is not about follow-through — it is about the commitment itself. Teach the commitment pause: "Before you say yes, ask yourself if you are actually going to do this." Also consider: does the student have the planning skills (IC1.1-B1) to manage their commitments? Accountability without organization produces the person who genuinely intends to keep their word but lacks the operational capacity to do so.

### 2. Group Accountability

**Developing well:** The student takes ownership of their contribution to group outcomes — including when the outcome is poor. In after-action reviews, they identify their own gaps without prompting. They speak up when they see problems in the group's plan rather than staying silent and distancing themselves from the eventual failure.

**Developing slowly:** The student blames others for group failures. Their contribution is described in terms of what they tried to do rather than what they actually delivered. They do not speak up about problems they observe — they wait for the failure and then say "I knew that was going to happen" without having raised the concern when it could have been addressed.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student have experience in genuinely collaborative groups where their contribution matters? If they have only experienced contrived group work where an adult manages the real coordination, they have not actually practiced group accountability. Also: does the student understand the difference between individual and group accountability? A student who did their part well but watched the group fail without intervening has a blind spot worth addressing.

### 3. Reputation Awareness

**Developing well:** The student can articulate what their pattern of behavior communicates to others. They understand that reliability, honesty, and follow-through create a picture in other people's minds — and that the picture is based on evidence, not on the student's self-image. They can identify discrepancies between how they see themselves and how others might see them based on their actual behavior.

**Developing slowly:** The student has no awareness that they have a reputation, or they believe their reputation should be based on their intentions rather than their actions. "But I am a good person" is offered as a defense when their behavior tells a different story. They are surprised or offended when others do not trust them, without recognizing that the distrust is evidence-based.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student received honest feedback from peers, not just authority figures? Peer feedback — "You said you would be there and you were not" — is often more impactful than mentor feedback because it comes from the social world the student cares about. If the student is insulated from peer consequences, consider creating contexts where their reliability is visible to peers, not just to you.

### 4. Consequences Over Excuses

**Developing well:** When the student falls short, their first response is a repair plan, not an explanation. They lead with "here is what I am doing about it" rather than "here is why it happened." Explanations, when offered, are brief, honest, and followed immediately by action.

**Developing slowly:** The student leads with explanations that are detailed, emotive, and designed to reduce the consequence rather than address the impact. The explanations get longer over time. The repair actions get shorter. The student has learned that a good enough explanation can substitute for follow-through.

**Diagnostic questions:** How do you respond to explanations? If a well-articulated excuse consistently reduces consequences, you are training the student to produce better excuses. The intervention: acknowledge the explanation briefly, then redirect to the repair. "I understand that happened. What is the plan?" Every time. The student learns that explanations are heard but do not change the obligation.

### 5. Trust Mechanics Understanding

**Developing well:** The student can describe how trust is built (consistency, truth-telling, follow-through over time), how it is damaged (broken commitments, lies, inconsistency), and how it is rebuilt (sustained changed behavior). They understand that rebuilding is slower than building, and that the asymmetry is structural, not punitive.

**Developing slowly:** The student views trust as a binary — you either trust someone or you do not — without understanding the gradient or the mechanics. They expect trust to be restored by apology alone, without sustained behavioral change. They are frustrated when others remain cautious after a single dramatic repair.

### 6. After-Action Accountability

**Developing well:** The student can conduct an honest after-action review — what happened, what was my role, what do I owe, what changes. They do this without excessive self-flagellation (which is its own form of performance) and without minimization. The review is matter-of-fact, specific, and forward-looking.

**Developing slowly:** After-action reviews produce either defensive justifications ("I did my best given the circumstances") or theatrical guilt ("I am the worst, I ruined everything") — neither of which is honest accountability. The first avoids ownership. The second substitutes emotional display for actual analysis.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling honest after-action reviews in your own life? When a family plan fails, do you conduct a brief, honest review — "Here is what I should have done differently" — or do you explain it away? The student learns the format from watching you do it.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Allowing the Commitment Pause to Become Avoidance
The commitment pause — "Before you say yes, make sure you mean it" — is a valuable tool. But some students will use it as permission to never commit to anything. "I am not sure I can do it" becomes the default response to every request. The pause is meant to improve the quality of commitments, not eliminate them. If the student is using it to avoid all risk, push them to commit — and then hold them to it.

### Letting Peer Accountability Replace Your Own
When the student enters group contexts, there is a temptation to let the group handle accountability. "The team will hold them accountable." Sometimes the team does. But at this age, peer accountability is inconsistent and often non-confrontational — peers are more likely to work around the unreliable member than to address the unreliability directly. Your accountability still matters. The commitment log, the weekly review, the after-action discussion — these are your tools, and they remain necessary even as the student's accountability arena expands beyond you.

### Confusing Busyness with Accountability
Some students become very busy — involved in many things, always moving, always doing. But busyness is not the same as accountability. A student who is part of six groups but delivers reliably in none of them is less accountable than a student who is part of two groups and delivers fully in both. Watch for the pattern of over-involvement without follow-through. The remedy is fewer commitments kept well, not more commitments kept poorly.

### The Reputation Image Problem
When you introduce reputation awareness, some students will hear "manage your image" rather than "build your character." They may begin performing reliability for an audience rather than practicing it as a standard. The tell: they are reliable when visibility is high and unreliable when no one is watching. If you observe this pattern, address it directly: "Your reputation is built by what you do when no one is looking, not by what you do when everyone is. Who are you when you think it does not count?"

### Excusing Failures with Feelings
At this age, emotional explanations become sophisticated: "I could not do it because I was feeling anxious." "I did not follow through because I was really stressed." Feelings are real and worth acknowledging. But feelings are not exemptions from accountability. The response: "I understand you were feeling anxious. That is real. And the commitment was real too. Both are true. The question is: what do you do about the commitment?" This validates the feeling without allowing it to erase the obligation.

### Not Following Through on Your Own Commitments
If you tell the student you will review their commitment log on Saturday and then you forget, you have just modeled the behavior you are trying to correct. Your follow-through on your commitments to the student is IC1.3 formation. Every kept promise builds your credibility as a mentor. Every broken one undermines it — and teaches the student that commitment-keeping is something adults talk about but do not practice.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Keeping Your Word
**Strong:** The student gives a specific example of a kept commitment that cost something — they showed up when they did not want to, they finished work when it was inconvenient. They also give a specific example of a broken commitment, describe the impact honestly, and identify what they learned.
**Weak:** Generalities. "I always keep my word." (Demonstrably untrue for any human.) Or inability to recall a specific instance of either keeping or breaking a commitment.

### Prompt 2: Reputation
**Strong:** The student can describe reputation as the sum of observed behavior. They give an honest assessment of their own — "People probably see me as [specific trait], because I tend to [specific behavior]." They understand that reputation is built by action, not by assertion.
**Weak:** "I have a good reputation because I am a good person." Self-assessment divorced from behavioral evidence. Or no awareness that reputation is a real thing that exists in other people's perceptions.

### Prompt 3: Group Failure
**Strong:** Specific incident. The student identifies their own contribution to the failure without hiding behind the group or performing excessive guilt. They describe what they learned and what they would do differently. They distinguish between their part and others' parts without using the others' failures to minimize their own.
**Weak:** "It was not my fault." Or conversely, "It was all my fault" (theatrical self-blame that avoids specificity). Or inability to recall a group failure — which suggests either limited group experience or a lack of self-awareness about their role in group dynamics.

### Prompt 4: Excuse vs. Response
**Strong:** The student can articulate the difference clearly and give an example of each from their own life. They can identify which they tend toward — "I usually make excuses first and have to catch myself" — with honesty about the pattern.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish between the two, or insists they never make excuses. (Everyone makes excuses. The question is whether you notice and correct.)

### Prompt 5: Trust Mechanics
**Strong:** Describes the building, damaging, and rebuilding of trust with specific examples. Understands the asymmetry — that building is slow and damaging is fast. Can identify someone they trust and articulate the behavioral basis for that trust.
**Weak:** Trust as a feeling rather than a prediction based on evidence. "I trust them because they are nice." No understanding of the behavioral mechanics.

### Prompt 6: Letting Someone Down
**Strong:** This is the hardest prompt because it requires vulnerability without self-pity. A strong response names a specific person who was counting on them, describes the specific failure, acknowledges the impact honestly, and describes what they did (or would do) to repair it. There is no attempt to contextualize the failure into acceptability.
**Weak:** Cannot or will not identify an instance. Or the instance is described entirely in terms of the student's feelings ("I felt so bad") rather than the other person's experience. Or the story ends with the explanation rather than the repair.
